{{wikidata link|Q17222452}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Ōta Shrine |Image = [[File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]]

{{wikidata link|Q17222452}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Ōta Shrine |Image = [[File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]]

Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]]

This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]]

{{wikidata link|Q17222452}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Ōta Shrine |Image = [[File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]]

{{wikidata link|Q17222452}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Ōta Shrine |Image = [[File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:pages]]

Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302|1=December 10}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591|1=Sesshū Gundan}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726|1=Harima no Kuni Fudoki}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808|1=Shimakami district}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004|1=Oda Haiji}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726|1=Harima no Kuni Fudoki}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808|1=Shimakami district}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004|1=Oda Haiji}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ota Shrine Ibaraki City}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:摂津国の式内社]] Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302|1=December 10}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591|1=Sesshū Gundan}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. (Ibaraki)}} [[JA:太田神社 (茨木市)]] {{wikidata link|Q17222452}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Ōta Shrine |Image = [[File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302|1=December 10}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591|1=Sesshū Gundan}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726|1=Harima no Kuni Fudoki}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808|1=Shimakami district}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004|1=Oda Haiji}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of (Ibaraki)}} [[JA:太田神社 (茨木市)]] {{wikidata link|Q17222452}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Ōta Shrine |Image = [[File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}} Ōta 3-chōme 15-1 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|50|39.03|N|135|34|36.86|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ōta Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} |Shintai = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate name = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=December 10|Q2302|WD=Q2302|1=December 10}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Ōta Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Ōta Shrine''' (おおだじんじゃ / おおたじんじゃ“Ōda”: Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines and local place names in Osaka Prefecture; “Ōta”: Nihon no Kamigami.) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Ōta, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === The current enshrined deities are the following three: * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Hayasusanō no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} As alternative theories regarding the enshrined deities, the *{{ill|lt=Jinja Kakuroku|Q11590708|WD=Q11590708|1=Jinja Kakuroku}}* lists Sarutahiko Ōkami, while the *{{ill|lt=Sesshū Gundan|Q11498591|WD=Q11498591|1=Sesshū Gundan}}* lists the three deities of Ise, Hachiman, and Sumiyoshi. In addition, because this area is thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}**Shinsen Shōjiroku*, Settsu Province, Shinbetsu Tenshin, Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji entry., there is also a theory that the original enshrined deity was {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|Q2564508|WD=Q2564508|lt=Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, the ancestral deity of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447|1=Nakatomi clan}}. === History === === Founding === [[File:Oodakf13.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Ōta Chausuyama Kofun and shrine precincts
(the shrine grove is on the left side of the kofun)}}{{Small|{{National land aerial photography}}.}}]] The date of founding is unknown. The shrine stands adjacent to the {{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}} (the Misima Aino Mausoleum of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}), and it is believed that the kofun existed before the founding of Ōta Shrine*Nihon no Kamigami*, Ōta Shrine entry.. Current archaeology holds that the true tomb of Emperor Keitai is the {{ill|lt=Imashirozuka Kofun|Q11376795|WD=Q11376795|1=Imashirozuka Kofun}} to the northeast, and the relationship between Ōta Shrine and the Ōta Chausuyama Kofun—including the question of who is actually buried there—has been noted. It is also thought to be the original homeland of the “Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji” recorded in the *{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}*, and there is a theory that rituals were conducted here by the Nakatomi Ōta clan*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry.. The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* states that the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji were descendants of Mimashuku Sukune, a thirteenth-generation descendant of Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto; this figure is sometimes identified with “Mimashi no Sukune no Mikoto” in the *Matsuo Shrine Genealogy* (there listed as a fourteenth-generation descendant), whose child “Ōta-hiko no Mikoto” is said to have led to the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji. Additionally, the *{{ill|lt=Harima no Kuni Fudoki|Q11498726|WD=Q11498726|1=Harima no Kuni Fudoki}}*, Ibo District, Ōta Village entry, records that “Kure no Suguri moved from the Korean Peninsula to Ōta Village in {{ill|lt=Kii Province|Q864053|WD=Q864053|1=Kii Province}}{{ill|lt=Nagusa District|Q11415547|WD=Q11415547|1=Nagusa district}}, then split off and moved to Ōta Village in Mishima Kamigun, Settsu Province.” Although this area is generally considered to belong to {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, which contradicts the mention of Mishima Kamigun ({{ill|lt=Shimakami District|Q10965808|WD=Q10965808|1=Shimakami district}}), the possession of the kabane title “Suguri” suggests immigration from Baekje. From this, a theory has been proposed that as the Nakatomi clan advanced into Mishima, they merged with descendants of these “Kure no Suguri,” forming the Nakatomi Ōta no Muraji, and that Ōta Shrine was founded as their clan shrine. === Overview === In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}* {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is listed under {{ill|lt=Shimashimo District|Q10976940|WD=Q10976940|1=Shimashimo district}}, Settsu Province, as “Ōta Shrine, Kuwabutsu”The Kujō family manuscript and Kongō-ji manuscript of the *Engishiki* record the name as “Ōda Shrine” (*Osaka-fu no Chimei*, Ōta Village entry).. This places it among the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and indicates that during the Kinensai festival the court presented hoes and quivers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}}), under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Ota 03.JPG|Main hall (right)
{{small|The Tenjin Shrine is on the left.}} File:Ōta-jinja (Ibaraki) torii.JPG|Torii gate === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === [[File:Ota 02.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|Inari Shrine}}]] * Tenjin Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}} * Inari Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami|1=Ukanomitama}} === Local information === '''Address''' * Ōta 3-chōme 15-1, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki City|Q502403|WD=Q502403|1=Ibaraki, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}} '''Nearby''' * Misima Aino Mausoleum of Emperor Keitai ({{ill|lt=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun|Q11444409|WD=Q11444409|1=Ōta Chausuyama Kofun}}) === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * Ibaraki City Board of Education, *Waga Machi Ibaraki: Shrines and Temples* (1989) {{National bibliography number|89049621}} * Katō Nezōichi, *Ōta-shi, Part 5* (1994) * *{{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}}: Place Names of Osaka Prefecture* ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), Ibaraki City, Ōta Village entry * Matsushita Akira, “Ōta Shrine,” in Tanigawa Ken’ichi (ed.), *Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Sites 3 – Settsu, Kawachi, Izumi, Awaji* ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}})) === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Ōta Haiji Ruins|Q113049004|WD=Q113049004|1=Oda Haiji}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Oda-jinja (Ibaraki)}} * [https://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/funai_jinja/dai2shibu/ibaraki-city/02032ohdajinja.html Ōta Shrine] – Osaka Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines {{Shinto footer}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oda Shrine (Ibaraki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:茨木市の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu
